data.title: e.l.f. Beauty's Bottom Has Been Well Defended - Growth/Tariff/ Debt Risks Remain (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
ELF Remains Attractively Valued Despite The Recent Recovery - Albeit Carrying Growth, Tariff, & Debt Risks

We previously covered e.l.f. Beauty, Inc. (ELF) in January 2025, discussing how its performance metrics continued to highlight its maturing growth profile in the US, with the International segment yet able to catch up.

While we had believed that the deep correction from July 2024 heights had been a boon, with the stock finally trading below its historical means and its peers, it was apparent that the stock was only suitable for those with a higher risk tolerance and long-term investing trajectory.

This was because it was uncertain when ELF's growth headwinds might bottom and growth might materialize, worsened by the higher short interest volumes.

ELF 1Y Stock Price

Since then, the bears have proven our bullish thesis wrong indeed, as ELF continues to pull back by -60.3% to finally find a floor at the $49s ranges, down from the October 2023 bottom of $100s, with it underscoring an extremely steep correction by -76.1% since the June 2024 peaks.

Most importantly, a similar stock price trend has been observed in its consumer discretionary/ beauty peers, partly attributed to the impacted consumer spending trends as the tariff war develops and the geopolitical tensions rise.

On the other hand, the 90 days tariff pause has already put a bottom to the market-wide meltdown, with the wider market also recovering by +23.2% since then, ELF included by +150%.

This is significantly aided by the management's promising tariff mitigation efforts, as observed in the ongoing diversification of its global production capacity away from China, down from the 2019 levels of nearly 100% to the current levels of 75%, with things to further moderate ahead:

We expect this will come down further by the end of fiscal 2026 as we continue to work with our key suppliers to set up additional operations outside the country. (Seeking Alpha)

Given the projected -$50M impact on its bottom-lines from the 55% import tariffs from China, ELF is also raising prices by +$1 across its entire product portfolio effective August 01, 2025, allowing them to mitigate some of the margin headwinds.

We expect the brand to remain highly popular amongst cost-conscious consumers as well, given the eventual average price point at approximately $7.50 (after the raised prices), compared to the $9.50 observed in the legacy mass cosmetics brands and over $20 in the prestige brands.

This is on top of ELF's intensified expansion efforts internationally, as observed in the robust international sales growth to $62.6M in FQ4'25 (+19% YoY) and $249.52M in FY2025 (+60.1% YoY), while building upon a year ago's performance at +115% YoY and +116.2% YoY, respectively.

Combined with the greater International sales ratio at 18.8% in FQ4'25 (+2.5 points YoY) and 18.9% in FY2025 (+3.7 points YoY), it is unsurprising that the management is looking to launch additional locations in the Netherlands, Belgium, and Poland, as "we aim to gain share in existing markets and expand into new markets."

This is significantly aided by ELF's recently announced acquisition, rhode, as the former grows its skin care ambitions beyond the prior Naturium acquisition, with both e.l.f. Skin and Naturium already comprising 18% of its overall sales by FQ3'25.

Even so, rhode's overall closing price of $1B implies a relatively elevated EV/ Revenue valuations of 4.71x, based on the LTM revenues of $212M ending March 31, 2025.

This is compared to ELF at the current levels of 4.34x, the Naturium acquisition at 3.94x (based on the acquisition price of $355M and 2023 revenues of $90M), the W3LL PEOPLE acquisition at 3.85x (based on the acquisition price of $27M and 2021 revenues of $7M), and the sector median of 1.53x.

Perhaps part of the premium valuations may be attributed to rhode's accelerated top-line growth from inception in 2022 to $212M by 2025 (based on only 10 products), compared to Naturium's inception in 2019 to $90M in 2023 (based on 49 products).

These may explain why ELF has bet its future skin care ambitions on these M&A activities, with Naturium's relatively premium average price point at $18s and rhode's at $27s implying the management's ambition to engage customers with deeper pockets:

I’ve been in the consumer space 34 years, and I’ve been blown away by seeing this brand over time. In less than three years, they’ve gone from zero to $212 million in net sales, direct-to-consumer only, with only 10 products. I didn’t think that was possible. [CNBC]

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons are likely why the consensus forward estimates remain somewhat promising, with ELF expected to chart an excellent top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +18.4%/ +15% through FY2028, respectively.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +14.5%/ +21%, albeit notably decelerating from the 5Y historical growth at a CAGR of +35.9%/ +40% and 9Y growth at +23.5%/ +31.2%, respectively.

These numbers imply ELF's maturing growth cadence, particularly in the US, based on the revenue growth by +0.5% YoY in FQ4'25 and +22.5% YoY in FY2025 compared to a year ago at +64.8% YoY and +71.3% YoY, respectively, with it remaining to be seen when its international revenue growth may catch up.

This is especially worsened by the likely to be expensive debt for the rhode acquisition, attributed to the higher for longer interest rates, the insufficient balance sheet at a net debt position of -$107.98M (-101.3% YoY), and the meager FY2025 net cash provided by operating activities of $133.84M (+88.1% YoY).

ELF Valuations

These reasons may also be why ELF has been discounted to FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 33.80x, down from the 5Y mean of 42.68x and nearing the 1Y mean of 32.04x, though up notably from the recent April 2025 bottom of 14.45x and the sector median of 16.06x.

Despite so, the stock remains somewhat compelling at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.18x, compared to the sector median of 2.78x and its consumer discretionary/ beauty peers, including:

  • Ulta Beauty (ULTA) at 1.76x,
  • L’Oréal S.A. (OTCPK:LRLCF) at 4.59x, and
  • Estée Lauder (EL) at 8.94x,

with it implying a decent margin of safety for those looking to add.

So, Is ELF Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

ELF 3Y Stock Price

For now, the bulls have finally swooped in to defend ELF's floor at the $49s ranges, with the stock rapidly breaking out of the 50/100/200 day moving averages to retest the next resistance levels of $120s ranges.

ELF's Overbought Levels

On the other hand, the triple digits recovery has directly contributed to ELF's seemingly overbought levels as observed in the technical indicators above, with it remaining to be seen if the stock will be able to sustain its upward momentum.

At the same time, we believe that there may be more volatility in the meantime, attributed to the drastically higher short interest volumes by +94.2% on a YoY basis and the rather greedy stock market sentiments at the time of writing.

Based on the recent April 2025 bottom P/E valuations of 14.45x and the FY2025 adj EPS of $3.39 (+6.6% YoY), it is apparent that the ELF stock is trading at a notable premium compared to our base-case fair-value estimates of $48.90.

Based on the consensus FY2028 adj EPS estimates of $5.15, there appears to be a minimal margin of safety to our base-case long-term price target of $74.40 as well.

Otherwise, based on the currently upgraded FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 33.80 (not too far from its peer group mean of 29.75x), there seems to be an excellent upside potential of +39.3% to our bull-case long-term price target of $174.00.

However, while ELF's trading floor may have materialized, aided by the bullish support observed in its recent recovery and the accretive acquisition trends, we believe that its potential volatility negates its growth investment thesis.

This is because the overbought stock is likely to moderately retrace to the prior 2023/ 2024 support levels of $100s ranges, with it implying a -19.9% downside from current levels.

Combined with the uncertainty arising from the lack of FY2026 guidance and the likely to be impacted FQ1'26 bottom-lines (since the price hikes will only be accretive to FQ2'26 numbers), we believe that some patience may be more prudent in the meantime.

data.title: AMG GW&K Municipal Bond Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The AMG GW&K Municipal Bond Fund (MUTF:GWMTX) (Class N) returned - 0.37% for the first quarter of 2025, compared with 0.26% for its benchmark, the Bloomberg 10-Year Municipal Bond Index. For the 12 months ending March 31, 2025, the Fund returned −0.04% versus 0.48% for the Index. Please note that this Fund has multiple share classes.

Market Review

Municipal bonds posted negative returns for the first quarter as heavy supply collided with weakening rollover demand late in the period. For much of January and February, municipal bonds held up reasonably well despite the elevated issuance, thanks to strong seasonal flows and sustained demand for attractive tax-exempt income. However, that tone faded in March as reinvestment slowed to a trickle and selling pressure picked up ahead of the April 15 tax-payment deadline. The long end took the brunt of the weakness over the quarter, struggling to keep pace with its shorter counterparts, even when the latter were rallying. The resulting curve steepening may reflect a return to more traditional demand patterns, where retail buyers gravitate toward the front end and steer clear of longer-dated maturities. Municipal/Treasury ratios, already stretched entering the year, offered little cushion in the sell-off, and headlines around potential tax-policy shifts may have added to the hesitation for some investors.

In the background, Treasury yields offered a window into the broader economic mood-short rates fell sharply as investors leaned into expectations for Federal Reserve Board (the Fed) cuts, while long-end yields declined more modestly, steepening the curve to its widest point in three years. Much of that reflected the market's struggle to make sense of evolving trade policy, as shifting tariff announcements introduced a fresh layer of uncertainty to the outlook. At times, it seemed clear that tariffs might weigh on growth and pull rates lower. At others, the inflationary implications received more attention, nudging long-term yields higher and dampening conviction around the pace of easing. While market odds for rate cuts increased over the quarter, they did so unevenly, with plenty of second-guessing along the way. The Fed largely mirrored that tension-pausing for now, but reiterating its expectation to cut rates later this year. As the quarter came to a close, trade policy emerged as the key wild card, and markets appeared to be adjusting to the possibility that priorities —and the responses they provoke—can shift without much warning.

Performance Review

The Fund underperformed the Bloomberg 10-Year Municipal Bond Index for the quarter. A significant allocation to bonds longer than the Index was the biggest negative as long rates rose in steepening fashion. A higher-quality bias was also a minor detractor. An allocation to bonds shorter than the Index benefited from a decline in front-end rates.

Strategy Review

Our trading activity in the first quarter focused on locking in higher yields for a longer period of time. For most of the quarter, that meant taking advantage of elevated new-issue supply, selectively participating in deals that offered strong relative value, particularly in call-protected maturities inside 15 years, where spreads and roll potential became increasingly compelling. Roughly 70% of our purchases came from the primary market, which included a wave of large offerings from New York issuers that priced with meaningful concessions. We also found value in electric utilities, hospitals, and higher education credits, which priced at spreads that stood out against other offerings. On the sell side, we continued a deliberate effort to rotate out of short-call structures, many of which had performed well in the prior cycle but were increasingly exposed to reinvestment risk. Sales were also concentrated in tight specialty state holdings, where spreads had compressed to levels that offered little future value for non-resident accounts. On balance, our trading activity pushed composite duration to its longest level in over a decade.

Investment Outlook

Municipal bonds enter the second quarter with higher yields, a steeper curve, and a market narrative increasingly shaped by uncertainty around inflation, growth, and trade. Near-term technicals still need to be worked through, but underlying demand for tax-exempt income remains firm, supported by stable credit fundamentals and cheaper valuations. Even amid recent volatility, municipal bonds continued to behave as you'd expect from one of the more stable corners of fixed income. The rise in 10-year municipal/Treasury ratios to over 75%, up from around 60% a year ago, has helped restore some of the relative value that had been missing for much of the past year. At the same time, curve steepening reflects both heightened inflation anxiety and a market still anticipating easing on a delayed timeline. Against this backdrop, we remain focused on opportunities to add selectively, especially where recent weakness has improved entry points without altering the long-term case for the asset class.

data.title: KOID: Investing In Humanoid Robotics And Embodied Intelligence At A Global Scale
data.description:
“We will make electricity so cheap that only the rich will burn candles.”

— Thomas Edison, December 31, 1879

Theme ETFs are notoriously late to the party, debuting well after the trend has peaked. However, the recent introduction of the KraneShares Global Humanoid and Embodied Intelligence Index ETF (NASDAQ:KOID) feels different. It feels almost prescient.

KOID is the first US-listed thematic equity ETF that captures the so-called “global humanoid opportunity.” The term “robot” was first coined by Karel Čapek in his 1920 play R.U.R (Rossum's Universal Robots), but the conceit, uncanny notion, or simple aspiration of a mechanically assembled humanoid of impressive intelligence has existed for millennium, reaching back beyond the Mechanical Turk to the precious inventions of Heron’s Alexandria.

However, it has only been over the past five years that we have seen the kind of accelerated convergence of AI, industrial IoT, and robotics which makes such an entity feel inevitable, not just as an industrial component but as a new participant in our social contract.

Adam Jonas, Morgan Stanley’s Head of Global Autos and Shared Mobility Research, recently penned an engaging report on the "Global Humanoid Model". He projects up to one billion humanoid robots by 2050, with major implications for global manufacturing, supply chains, and geopolitics.

On a global level, the analyst sees the segment ultimately eclipsing the auto industry in scale, with final equipment sales reaching double the revenues of today’s top eighteen automakers combined.

The ETF

Initiated earlier this month (June 5, 2025), the KraneShares Global Humanoid and Embodied Intelligence Index ETF has an expense ratio of 0.69% and a tiny AUM of $3.23 million. (To drive investment, there is a 0.10% waiver on the expense ratio until August 1, 2025, and after that new entrants will be charged 0.79%.)

Unlike existing robotic ETFs, KOID invests in the so-called “humanoid” branch of robotics and embodied AI. It is positioning itself to capture that new market of industrial, commercial, and domestic use cases that comes with a more adroit, teachable system.

KOID invests in the full spectrum of enabling technologies that form the foundation of humanoid development. These include:

  • Humanoid integration & manufacturing
  • Actuation systems (providing the “muscles” of the robot)
  • Mechanical systems
  • Sensing and Perception (LiDAR, tactile, vision data, etc.)
  • Semiconductors & Technology
  • Critical materials

In their prospectus, KraneShares astutely describes this ecosystem as simply those intelligent systems integrated into physical machines that can sense, learn, and interact with the real world. (Elsewhere it has been called “physical AI”.)

Humanoid robots are thus a subset of embodied intelligence, with their human-like size astutely designed to work seamlessly in already populated environments.

At present, the fund's top ten holdings are:

KOID offers full international exposure, with holdings in companies based primarily in the United States, China, Australia, and Japan. Top holding Jabil Inc. (JBL) is a Florida-based manufacturer that has seen dramatic growth in its Intelligent Infrastructure segment lately due to accelerating AI-driven demand. CT-based Amphenol Corp (APH)—one of the largest companies in electrical connection solutions, sensors, and antennas—is also enjoying the datacenter buildout.

Nvidia (NVDA) is obviously well-known, with its chips being a critical aspect of "endowed intelligence," but in this ETF's context, it also provides microservices and SDKs for deploying AI features (as seen with "project Maxine").

Regal Rexnord (RRX) specializes in the "muscles" part of the robot and in early May its CEO specifically talked up the humanoid demand for its products, with secured contracts worth over $20 million annually and a possible funnel of opportunities totaling approximately $100 million. Key product offerings include motors, bearings, and actuators. Also involved in mechanical systems: Melexis NV is a Belgium-based company that develops mixed-signal integrated circuits and Rainbow Robotics, an impressive Korean “cobot” maker.

Among the top ten are MP Materials (MP) and Lynas Rare Earths (LYC), mining outfits from the US and Australia respectively. Focused on the strategic metals at work in physical AI systems (and high-tech more broadly), they will be buffeted by US-China diplomacy but certainly benefit from Trump's nearshoring agenda.

The ETF follows a specific index—the MerQube Global Humanoid and Embodied Intelligence Index—which rebalances quarterly and has rather wide parameters. Though the fund has been up for less than a month, the index has more than five years of performance and is up 200%+ since December 2019:

To be included as a KOID holding, a publicly traded company can be from a DM or an EM, but it has to have a $1 billion market cap when added to the index (and will be dropped if it falls below $800 million).

Future Rollout

"The future is already here; it's just not very evenly distributed."

— William Gibson, Cyberpunk Novelist Extraordinaire

Estimates from Morgan Stanley suggest humanoid robots generate up to $5 trillion in world revenue by 2050.

Heady stuff, but I will emphasize that most analysts see the shift as gradual and focused on manufacturing/warehouse settings. By 2035, there are expected to be about 13 million humanoid robots in use, most of which will be used in industrial and commercial settings. By one estimate, humanoid robots could replace a mere 40,000 jobs by 2030. It ramps up from there, however: by 2040 the number goes to 8.4 million, and ten years later, 62.7 million. Even by 2050 when MS estimates their “1 billion humanoid robots” will be in use, an estimated 90% will be siloed in industrial and commercial environments.

Tesla (TSLA) is presently the ETF’s seventh-largest holding, not for its cars but for its Optimus segment. It seems premature but inevitable. There is a strong argument that those companies which provide “vertical integration” and offer the “brains, bodies, branding and ecosystems” of the robots, will be in the so-called pole position. Like Korea’s Rainbow or US privately held Figure AI, Tesla offers something akin to this via its Optimus program. It is an adroit, teachable product that can produced at an extreme scale.

During a March meeting, Elon Musk expressed the idea that Optimus could become a category-defining product and that Tesla was aiming to acquire enough resources to produce 10,000 to 12,000 Optimus robots in 2025. He went to waver a bit:

“Even 5,000 robots, that’s the size of a Roman legion, FYI, which is like a little scary thought. Like a whole legion of robots, I’ll be like ‘whoa’. But I think we will literally build a legion, at least one legion of robots this year, and then probably 10 legions next year. I think it’s kind of a cool unit, you know? Units of legion. So probably 50,000-ish next year.”

At present, it is generally understood that most robotic companies begin with narrowly scoped pilots, achieving early wins by solving high-friction, ergonomically difficult, repetitive, or dangerous roles in particular deployments. Later, they may evolve into a “Robot as a Service” / RaaS-based operation. Adoption will obviously be a condition of price. Leasing options and servicing costs will also be an aspect.

Along with AI, Beijing is leaning into the mass production of humanoid robots. It syncs well with their “Made in China 2025” modernization strategies and the projected strain of an aging demographic.

Several Chinese companies at the forefront — Unitree Robotics, AgiBot, Galbot, Engine AI, and Leju Robotics — are truly driving the sector, which is projected to reach a market value of approximately 4.5 billion yuan (US$616 million) this year on the mainland.

China has developed an interesting lead. (Allegedly, the Ubtech Walker S1 can be bought on Alibaba for $24,500 and the Unitree G1 costs a mere $16,000 for light manufacturing tasks.) According to Sheng Zhong, Morgan Stanley’s Head of Industrials Research:

“It is becoming apparent that national support for ‘embodied AI’ may be far greater in China than in any other nation, driving continued innovation and capital formation. In our opinion, China's lead in AI-robotics may need to widen before rivals, including the U.S., pay closer attention."

In the ETF, Chinese companies don’t make the top ten holdings, but there are several of them “below the fold” in the full holdings. The international aspect of the holdings (and KraneShares as an outfit that knows China very well) is something I find quite interesting about this fund.

Risks

“A man is something that feels happy, plays the piano, likes going for a walk, and in fact, wants to do a whole lot of things that are really unnecessary.”

― Karel Čapek, R.U.R, 1920

The risks to the humanoid trend are myriad. A rare earth embargo by Beijing could strangle development. Low-cost Chinese competitors could bankrupt the West’s efforts at automation (which looks comparatively expensive and niche) and seize control of most markets outside of the US and Europe.

More broadly: though robots are bridging labor gaps, reducing costs, and improving safety, they will continue to require careful integration with human workflows to be viable.

There are case studies of humanoids delivering quantifiable operational and financial value, from Amazon’s Digit warehouses to BMW’s production lines and robots that may help address our urgent global challenges: aging populations, elder care, and labor shortages in tough, dirty work across industries. However, their very presence could become a contested ethical battlefield.

The following is a joke my father told me many, many years ago:

Setting: It’s 1910 and an engineer and labor unionist are staring down at a new-fangled steam shovel operating at a Manhattan building site.

The labor unionist turns and says: “By God, if it were not for that infernal machine, I would have a hundred men with shovels down there in that pit, making good wages for their families….”

The engineer replies: Or a thousand men with teaspoons….”

The labor unionist’s line touches your heart — families, hunger, poverty are at stake — but the engineer’s next line hits you with the proverbial long view and, yes, the absurdity of using tools that have ceased to be efficient.

Work’s position as a source of meaning and self-worth is serious third-rail stuff. Work feeds our social dignity; it nourishes the soul. Sinclair Lewis' famously line: “it is difficult to get a man to understand something when his salary depends upon his not understanding it”—comes to mind. I could imagine legislation emerging in certain societies in the 2030s that will dramatically curtail humanoid robotics in specific sectors of the economy.

The immense complexity of humanoids still makes them an expensive product, with Morgan Stanley Research estimating that, at least in high-income countries, the cost of one humanoid hovered at $200,000 in 2024.

However, they estimate that prices will likely fall to about $150,000 in three years and $50,000 by 2050. They even speculate that 10% of U.S. households overall could have a humanoid by 2050, with the penetration rate ranging from 3% for families earning $50,000 to $75,000 a year, to 33%, for those with annual income above $200,000.

The social nature of this intrusion and the evolving class divide will be profound. Indeed, the humanoid robot as a category could spawn a full-scale societal rejection.

Brian Merchant's Blood in the Machine chronicles the Luddites who rose up rather than starve at the factory owners who were using automation to erase their livelihoods. More recently, the total erasure of Google Glass in 2014 came in large part from nothing more than the social, stigmatizing revulsion of “Glassholes” at San Francisco bars and cafés.

Conclusion

These big risks and ethical issues aside, from a portfolio standpoint, KOID is a useful, long-term investment vehicle. It has an interesting mandate and leans into the future. Its wide international purview and focus on an enduring trend make it an ETF worthy of your due diligence.

data.title: Rotation Into FTSE 250 Shows Confidence On A U.K. Recovery
data.description:
By Sandrine Soubeyran, Director, Global Investment Research

This insight is a follow-up to a previous note in February, where we highlighted the relative value and opportunities of the UK equity market.

The UK equity market continues to show its resilience amid an increasingly complex geopolitical and economic landscape. Over twelve months, the FTSE 100 Index has returned nearly 20%, outperforming the US (~12%) and the World ex US (~16%) equity markets, all in US dollar terms.

Its heavy exposure to defensive sectors, like Financials and Consumer Staples, which account for nearly 41% of the market, has shielded the UK equity market from weak economic growth and allowed it to benefit from a global rotation into value stocks.

Revival of the UK domestic market

The FTSE 100, home to large-cap, globally diversified companies, has also outperformed smaller UK companies over the year. Accordingly, the FTSE 100 has risen by 12% in sterling terms, compared to about 9% for the mid and small-cap UK indices (see left chart).

However, the last three months have seen another notable change. Investors have not only sought exposure to larger UK companies but are also embracing the broader UK equity market. The FTSE 250, often regarded as a barometer for the UK’s economic health, represents companies, which are lower down in the capitalisation levels compared to the FTSE 100. Stronger-than-expected UK economic growth in Q1 (though there was a tariff effect in March and a much softer month of April), underpinned by easing inflation and lower interest rates, has rekindled interest for domestically oriented companies.

This has resulted in a reversal in market leadership and the outperformance of the FTSE 250 and FTSE SmallCap (up by about 9%) relative to the FTSE 100, which has returned 5% during the same period (see right chart).

Recent UK fund flows privilege UK equities and UK smaller companies over GBP bonds

The deepening interest for UK smaller companies is also evident in the recent Lipper fund flows. Investors have switched out of sterling bond funds, notably GBP government bond funds, into UK equity funds since ‘Liberation Day’ in early April, including a more recent rotation into UK smaller and mid-cap equity markets.

Financials drive the performance of FTSE 100 and FTSE 250 over 3M

Breaking down the return contribution by sectors shows the FTSE 100 and FTSE 250 Financials, Industrials and Consumer Staples providing high contribution to performance over the last three months. But the higher weight of Financials in FTSE 250 (45%) largely explains the mid-cap index outperformance relative to the FTSE 100 (25%). The Industrial weight is also slightly larger in the FTSE 250 versus FTSE 100. Moreover, the underperformance of healthcare weighed on the FTSE 100, which has a higher weight in pharmaceuticals.

Geographic revenue exposure exposes the domestic slant of the FTSE 250

The geographic revenue exposure of UK-listed companies highlights sharp differences between the FTSE 100 and FTSE 250. The FTSE 100 derives nearly 30% of its revenue from the United States, far outstripping contributions from other regions such as Japan and China. This underscores the critical role of the US economy in driving earnings for UK-listed companies. The geographic revenue exposure for the FTSE 250, unsurprisingly, shows much more dependence on the domestic economy, with 43% of its revenue exposure coming from the UK. Although considerably lower than firms in the FTSE 100, FTSE 250 companies still derive 10% of their revenues from the United States, even if the overseas exposure is more modest than for FTSE 100 companies.

Overall, the UK market offers a blend of defensive characteristics and income appeal. The FTSE 100 remains well-suited to those seeking stability and yield, while the FTSE 250 may appeal to investors wanting exposure to a domestic recovery. In an era of heightened uncertainty, balancing exposure to UK equities with global diversification with a domestic slant could provide some interesting long-term opportunities.

data.title: 7 Steps To Create A New High-Dividend Index
data.description:
By Kathleen Dobyns, Senior Manager, Product Management

If they are reliable payers, high-dividend stocks can help investors in two ways: they boost the income from an equity portfolio while offering potential capital appreciation.

Building a high-income equity portfolio requires sophistication

In today's uncertain economic landscape, financial advisors face increasing pressure to deliver portfolios that offer both growth potential and a consistent stream of income. Amid market volatility, inflation pressures, concentration risks, and shifting client demographics, dividend-yielding stocks offer advisors a powerful tool to help clients achieve goals like stable retirement income, inflation protection, and diversified portfolio construction.

Stocks with higher dividend yields often represent mature businesses with sustainable earnings and disciplined management—precisely the type of investment that can provide an anchor of stability in volatile market conditions. However, advisors who simply chase the highest dividend yields risk exposing their clients to capital losses and income disruption. These abnormally high yields (often called "yield traps") can be a precursor of dividend cuts: Traders usually anticipate such cuts by marking down share prices, and dividend yields are calculated using backward-looking data.

A more strategic high-income equity solution captures the benefits of dividend stocks, while avoiding the pitfalls of overly simplistic approaches. To meet growing investor interest in these smarter strategies, FTSE Russell launched the FTSE Global Equity High Income index series in May 2024. The index series uses a simple, capitalisation-weighted methodology to reflect the performance of global stocks with relatively high tax-adjusted dividend yields, while also addressing concerns about possible yield traps.

Seven construction steps

High-dividend indices vary in the complexity of their construction methodologies. For this index series, we’ve adopted a simple rank-and-select rule to choose constituents. The index series follows six intuitive design steps:

  • Use forward-looking dividend statements. We use forecast dividends rather than trailing (historic) dividends to rank potential index constituents; although there is close alignment between the two measures and past dividends are often a reliable indicator of future payments, forecast dividends cover almost all the global equity markets and are more responsive to changes in market conditions.

Forecast dividend coverage by cumulative index constituent weights

  • Look at after-tax dividend yields. We rank potential index constituents by net-of-tax yield, recognising the fact that index users often receive dividends after a deduction for withholding tax.
  • Avoid stocks with no dividends or negative returns. To reduce the risk of yield traps, we exclude stocks with zero forecast or trailing dividends and those in the bottom fifth percentile of companies with negative one-year returns. This step screens out some of the stocks most at risk of cutting dividends.
  • Consider global exposure. To enable easier country or regional carve-outs of the index series, we rank stocks within each of the eight regional universes for the FTSE Global Equity Index Series (Asia Pacific ex China ex Japan, China, Japan, Developed Europe, Emerging Europe, Middle East & Africa, Latin America, and North America).
  • Stick to the top 50% of dividend payers in each region. To ensure relatively low concentration of risk, maintain diversification, and aid investability, we select the top 50% of securities by dividend yield within each regional universe and weight them by market capitalisation.
  • Perform regular maintenance. To constrain turnover while ensuring regular index maintenance, we incorporate buffer rules and rebalance the index annually, with the quarterly removal of securities with zero forecast or trailing dividends.

A near-doubling of the index dividend yield

Applying these construction steps to the FTSE All-World index (via a back-test) resulted in a consistent near-doubling of the index’s dividend yield between September 2002 and March 2025, highlighting the selection criteria’s potential value for income-seeking investors.

FTSE All-World and FTSE All-World Equity High Income index dividend yields (%)

Return and risk statistics

The total returns of the FTSE All-World Equity High Income and the FTSE All-World indices were broadly similar for most of the 23-year period, with the FTSE All-World Equity High Income index returning 8.91% per annum between September 2022 and March 2025 and the FTSE All-World index returning 9.80% per annum over the period.

In fact, the two indices’ total return ratio stayed close to 100 between 2002 and 2017, after which the outperformance of select low-dividend or zero-dividend stocks, particularly in the US equity market’s tech sector, helped the FTSE All-World index to pull ahead. The gap narrowed in 2024 as certain major tech companies began paying dividends.

Notably, the FTSE All-World Equity High Income index showed lower annualised volatility than the FTSE All-World index, reinforcing the idea that higher-dividend stocks are often stable businesses with strong cash flows that tend to be somewhat less risky than the broader market.

Total return comparison: FTSE All-World Equity High Income index/FTSE All-World index

Total return p.a. (2002-2024) Standard deviation p.a. (2002-2024)
FTSE All-World Index 9.80% 15.31%
FTSE All-World Equity High Income Index 8.91% 14.65%

Source: FTSE Russell, data from 30/9/2002-31/3/2025, 30/9/2002=100. data.title: ZLB:CA: A High-Quality Alternative To The Canadian Large-Cap Universe
data.description:
All values are in CAD unless noted otherwise.

We have covered the Canadian utility giants, Hydro One Ltd (OTCPK:HRNNF) (H:CA), Emera Inc. (EMA) (EMA:CA) and Fortis Inc. (FTS) (FTS:CA) a few times on this platform. Another thing they have in common is that all three have a beta of less than 1, i.e. their price does not move as much as the broader market.

In this sub 1 beta, the stock price in fact moves less, making these a safe haven for risk-averse investors. The five-year-old in me loves the explanation of this concept provided by Investopedia:

By definition, the market, such as the S&P 500, has a beta of 1.0. Every stock has a beta that indicates how widely its price swings in comparison with the market as a whole. A stock that swings more than the market over time has a beta above 1.0. A stock that moves less than the market will have a beta of less than 1.0.

The low volatility relative to the market makes the aforementioned trio perfect candidates for the BMO Low Volatility Canadian Equity ETF (TSX:ZLB:CA). Unsurprisingly, they occupied a spot in the top 10 holdings of this ETF at the end of last month.

With around 50 holdings in total, the top 10 made up a third of the portfolio. ZLB:CA selects its portfolio incumbents from the pool of Canadian large cap stocks. The process incorporates a rule-based methodology to pick the relatively thick-skinned (read low beta) stocks for its close to $5 billion dollar portfolio. The idea according to the fund website is to provide a product that caters to investors looking to build a portfolio of diversified large cap stocks, with a low- to medium-risk profile, and the potential for long-term capital appreciation. The website also notes that ZLB:CA's portfolio is reconstituted in November and rebalanced in May. This ETF has an annual expense of 0.39%, of which 0.35% is for management fees. While owning this fund will not break the bank, it is a tad higher than the plain vanilla index ETFs.

ZLB makes quarterly distributions of 28 cents, and that amount has remained unchanged since 2023.

With ETF price at $53, the $1.12 annual distribution gets us to a yield of 2.11%. Large cap companies rarely pay out a hefty dividend, so ZLB's yield is in line with expectations of holding these companies. We pulled up the companies included in ZLB's top 10 and majority yield sub 2%.

Fortis and Quebecor yield over 3% and Emera is the most generous at close to 5%. Formed in 2011, this fund does not aim to satisfy the monthly income cravings of its unitholders. However, it does intend to provide a potential for capital appreciation with low volatility stocks. Next we see how that has played out over the years.

Performance

The iShares S&P/TSX 60 Index ETF (XIU:CA) was established in 1990, making it the first exchange-traded fund in the world, according to its website. No, we did not change the protagonist mid-article. We will merely be comparing ZLB:CA's performance with XIU:CA, as the former holds Canadian large caps too. XIU:CA yields around 2.70%, so slightly more than its younger counterpart. Both have provided a good return to their unitholders over the last decade.

Our lower yielding protagonist has actually edged out XIU:CA. ZLB:CA provides a comparison to its benchmark, the S&P/ TSX Capped Composite Index in their Annual Management Report of Fund Performance (MRFP) and the 10-year comparison is slightly better than the index, versus the shorter time frames. Note, the comparison below is versus the actual index and not the XIU:CA ETF.

The 10-year performance vis a vis the Index is even more impressive when we consider that the ETF has expenses (0.39%/year) versus none for the Index. The variance with the Index for 2024 was actually addressed in the MRFP:

The difference in the performance of the ETF relative to the Index during the Period (-6.31%) resulted from the management expense ratio (-0.39%), and the impact of certain other factors (-5.92%), which may have included timing and holding differences from the concentration of portfolio holdings in low volatility stocks versus the Index, and market volatility.

Source: Annual MFRP

Notwithstanding the underperformance versus the Index in 2024, 15.34% as a standalone return is nothing to scoff at. So we can see that this fund does deliver the capital appreciation with a portfolio of low volatility stocks. Does that make it a buy in our books?

Verdict

While the low volatility index works in theory, we need to see whether it works in practice. As the wise saying goes:

...in theory there is no difference between theory and practice, but in practice there is.

If we see the drawdown in the 2020 COVID-19 meltdown, there was some outperformance, but it was marginal.

Perhaps some might say that COVID-19 is not the most perfect test. In the 2022 bear market, ZLB:CA did do very well. One point to stress though was that even XIU:CA and the TSX index did not really experience a huge drawdown.

Nonetheless, we cannot really deduct any points from this fund. Most funds that sell volatility via options give up huge amounts of upside with nothing to show for it in bear markets. At least here, the fund is matching or exceeding the upside and giving some outperformance in down markets. It is impossible to find fault with that. We recommend this to those that want an index like move with some alpha generation in strong bear markets.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints. Most of our articles are not written for "buy and hold" investors. Our articles are definitely not written for investors who ignore capital preservation while focusing only on yield.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Okta's Bottoming Momentum As The Bulls Defend Its Uptrend Support Line
data.description:
OKTA Is Even More Attractive After The Recent Pullback, With Historical Trends Implying Further Upsides Ahead

We previously covered Okta (NASDAQ:OKTA) in March 2025, discussing how it continued to outperform expectations, thanks to the stock's previously discounted valuations compared to its historical means and its cybersecurity peers.

Combined with the company's robust performance metrics in FQ4'25, the excellent FY2026 guidance, and the richer balance sheet, we had believed that its investment thesis remained compelling with high double digits capital appreciation prospects.

OKTA 1Y Stock Price

Since that reiterated Buy rating upon a moderate retracement, OKTA has indeed corrected to our Buy Zones of between $100s and $90s, thanks to the market's over-reaction to the new US administration's tariff war, with a similar correction also observed in the wider market and the cybersecurity sector in April 2025.

Since then, thanks to the 90 days tariff pause, it is unsurprising that market sentiments have recovered as observed in the stock prices' quick rebound, aided by the durable enterprise SaaS spending trends no matter the uncertain macroeconomic environment and the tariff risks.

The same has been observed in the cybersecurity sector, with OKTA already raising their FY2026 adj operating income guidance to $715M at the midpoint (+21.8% YoY) and adj EPS to $3.255 (+15.8% YoY), despite the stable top-line guidance at $2.855B (+9.3% YoY).

This is compared to the prior numbers of $710M (+20.9% YoY) and $3.175 (+12.9% YoY) offered in the FQ4'25 earnings call, respectively, while building upon the double beat FQ1'26 performance.

These numbers are well supported by OKTA's growing multi-year Remaining Performance Obligations [RPO] of $4.08B (-3% QoQ/ +21.4% YoY) as well.

These underscore the robust Enterprise demand for its workforce and identity security offerings as its customer count with over $100K in spending also grows to 4.87K (+1.4% QoQ/ +7% YoY/ +233.5% from FY2020 levels of 1.46K).

It is apparent from these developments that OKTA remains capable to grow adoption based on the somewhat consistent net new customer additions at +350 QoQ/ +900 YoY as well, compared to a year ago at +150 QoQ/ +1,050 YoY.

This is significantly aided by the seemingly bottoming dollar-based net retention rate at 106% (-1 points QoQ/ -5 YoY/ -13 from FY2020 levels of 119%), with it implying the improving up-selling and cross selling trends thanks to the new product launches, with the latter already contributing over $400M in incremental annualized contract value as they also "land new customers."

Combined with the excellent pricing power from the growing subscription adj gross profit margins of 83.9% (+0.4 points QoQ/ +0.4 YoY/ +1.9 from FY2020 levels of 82%), we can understand why OKTA has raised their FY2026 bottom-line guidance indeed.

Much of the tailwinds are naturally attributed to the growing adoption of Agentic AI platforms, which has triggered a "newfound excitement on the customer identity side," with more Enterprises already looking to adopt vertically integrated and scalable security solutions for human and non-human bad actors.

These reasons are also why OKTA's management has hinted at further innovations and new product launches targeting the "whole agentic revolution," with the additional focus on "service accounts and tokens and API access that's actually tracking the agent and knowing what that means and knowing what security posture you want and what governance lifecycles" likely to be accretive to its long-term growth prospects.

Risk Warning

The Consensus Forward Estimates

For now, OKTA's decelerating growth profile has been exemplified by the downgraded consensus forward estimates, with the company expected to report a top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +10.1%/ +13% through FY2028.

This is compared to the prior estimates of +13.9%/ +18.4% (a year ago) and the historical 5Y top-line growth at a CAGR of +34.8%, with it underscoring its maturing growth profile - a trend similarly observed in many of its peers, including CrowdStrike Holdings (CRWD).

OKTA Valuations

These reasons are likely why OKTA has been moderately discounted to FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 29.79x, compared to the 1Y mean of 32.41x, the 3Y mean of 49.22x, the 5Y mean of 624.84x, and its cybersecurity peer group mean of ~69x, including:

  • CRWD at 140.71x,
  • Sentinel (S) at 90.11x
  • Fortinet (FTNT) at 41.67x,
  • Palo Alto (PANW) at 62.40x, and
  • Zscaler (ZS) at 97.71x.

Combined with OKTA's moderating dollar-based net retention rate on a QoQ/ YoY basis, it remains to be seen when the Agentic AI boom may trigger its renewed growth profile, as observed in the reiterated FY2026 revenue guidance $2.855B (+9.3% YoY) compared to a year ago's performance at +15.4% YoY.

These reasons are also why the stock is only suitable for those with a longer investing trajectory, since its growth materialization may be prolonged, as observed in its discounted valuations.

On the other hand, thanks to the double digits adj EPS growth prospects, we believe that OKTA remains cheap at a FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.49x as well, compared to CRWD at 2.63x, FTNT at 3.05x, PANW at 3.03x, and ZS at 5.24x, aside from S at 0.79x.

This underscores the excellent margin of safety for those still looking to add, significantly aided by its increasingly richer SaaS cash flow story, based on the FQ1'26 net cash provided by operating activities at $241M (+10% YoY) and FY2025 at $750M (+46.4% YoY).

So, Is OKTA Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

OKTA 3Y Stock Price

For now, OKTA has been charting higher highs and higher lows since the October 2022 bottoms, as similarly observed in the stock's recently established uptrend support lines since October 2024.

This is also why our previous Buy rating upon the materialization of our Buy zone of between the $100s and $90s ranges has worked out as intended, with the stock's recent pullback from May 2025 heights in the $127s to $98s by the time of writing being inline with its historical trading trends.

Despite the consensus lowered FY2028 adj EPS estimates to $4.06 and the discounted FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 30.17x (compared to its cybersecurity peer group mean of ~69x), OKTA continues to offer a promising upside potential of +24.6% to our base-case long-term price target of $122.40.

Otherwise, we believe that the market may potentially upgrade OKTA nearer to its 3Y mean of ~49x, with it likely to trigger a speculative more than doubling upside potential to our bull-case long-term price target of $198.90.

This conjecture is not overly aggressive in our view, based on the consistent upgrades in its valuations from the September 2024 bottom of 26.71x and the April 2025 bottom of 28.50x, along with the consistent double beat performances for the past 16 consecutive quarters and the likely to be raised FY2026 guidance ahead.

OKTA's Oversold Levels

At the same time, the bulls have defended OKTA's recent floor at the $98s ranges as observed in the technical indicators above, with historical trends implying an upward momentum ahead as similarly suggested by the stock's uptrend movement thus far.

Combined with the excellent performance metrics, the growing multi-year RPOs, and the cheap valuations, we are re-iterating our Buy rating here.

Do not miss this dollar cost averaging dip.

data.title: Free Cash Flow: An All-Weather Equity Strategy
data.description:
By Emerald Yau, Head of Equity Index Product Management, Asia

In today’s market, where frequent policy shifts strike like unexpected villains, equity investors are often forced to adopt protective gear in a hurry. Strategies anchored in value stocks, dividend plays and other traditionally defensive equity sectors have been some of the obvious ways to protect against market turbulence.

But there is another strategy in the background, often overlooked but ready to save the day: quality companies with high free cash flow. These companies may be less well-known, but in times of uncertainty they may have the financial firepower to hold the line.

Why companies generating high free cash flow deserve a bigger role in portfolios

Free cash flow (FCF) is viewed as one of the most revealing indicators of a company’s financial health. In essence, free cash flow is the true cash a company generates (after accounting for the capital expenditures it needs to maintain its core operations).

Free Cash Flow = Operating Cash Flow − Capital Expenditures

This represents the money that a company can deploy freely — to pay dividends, buy back shares, reduce debt, invest in growth and innovation and more — all key attributes for investors.

Companies with strong and consistent free cash flows are typically associated with better financial health and they can respond more quickly to competitive pressures. Their financial strength and flexibility may be an advantage in times of turbulence.

FTSE Cash Flow Focus Index Series: an all-weather investment strategy

To fight off the unexpected villains in today’s market, equity investors need more than just hope. They need a resilient, all-weather strategy.

The FTSE Cash Flow Focus Index Series, launched in 2024, offers a transparent, index methodology centred on capturing high-quality, financially resilient companies with strong free cash flow profiles.

These companies can offer a triple benefit: a defensive stance during downturns, capital appreciation potential during upswings, and more reliable dividends. This combination makes them well-rounded partners for long-term investors through fast-changing market conditions.

In this article, we’ll delve into the characteristics that could make the FTSE Cash Flow Focus indices a prudent choice for equity strategies in uncertain times.

Defensive shield: protecting against market turbulence

Companies with high free cash flow are typically less reliant on external financing to fund their operations. Their lower debt burden means there’s a higher probability that they can march forward even in times of economic stress, providing an overall defensive shield to the FTSE Cash Flow Focus indices.

The above-average cash position also positions these companies to combat unexpected challenges, such as policy changes or supply chain disruptions. The ability to quickly pivot, invest in solutions, or expand into new markets without requiring external capital therefore gives these companies an advantage in maintaining competitive positioning.

For example, as shown in Exhibit 1 (based on a back-test of the index), the Russell 1000 Cash Flow Focus index, which was launched in 2025 and contains 100 cash-rich US large caps, would have demonstrated remarkable power of resilience during periods of market stress – from the global financial crisis and the Covid-19 shock, to the 2022 correction and recent policy-driven volatility. Since 2006, the index gave a total return of 251% higher than the base universe, the Russell 1000 — thanks in part to the cash-rich constituents’ ability to shield and defend.

Exhibit 1: Relative performance of the Russell 1000 Cash Flow Focus Index

Speed burst: keeping pace with market rallies

The power of the FTSE Cash Flow Focus indices is not limited to their defensiveness. Capturing upside potential has been another effective advantage.

Too good to be true? Not really. Here’s why.

Cash-rich companies have great flexibility to reinvest in their business, fund strategic initiatives, acquire competitors or explore new markets. These moves can boost competitiveness by fueling the power of innovation. In doing so, they may also drive higher revenue growth and, potentially, stock price appreciation.

Dividend armour: strengthening security measures

Cash-rich companies also have the ability to provide added security to dividends.

Companies that generate significant cash flows are typically better positioned to weather economic downturns and market shocks, raising the likelihood they can continue paying dividends even when other companies might be forced to cut or suspend payouts.

For income-focused investors, sustainable dividend payments – even in challenging times – can be particularly valuable. By representing a combination of cash-rich companies, the FTSE Cash Flow Focus indices are thus equipped with the potential to bring an appealing, alternative option to investors seeking dividend income.

data.title: H.B. Fuller Just Got Leaner And Smarter, And That's Bullish
data.description:
Thesis

My research argues that H.B. Fuller Company (FUL) is executing a deliberate approach in favor of a tighter, higher-margin portfolio. The Q2 2025 results show that a shift taking shape, with the company steering its adhesives and sealants toward big-ticket trends like infrastructure upgrades, clean energy, and advanced materials, instead of chasing volume.

Highlights from H.B. Fuller's Q2 2025 Results

What looks like routine corporate shuffling at H.B. Fuller may actually be a calculated move, timed to catch a shift in the industry. The market seemed to think so, shares jumped nearly 11% after the company’s latest earnings report. Management, staring down a $80 billion global adhesives market, has decided the game of chasing every last dollar of volume isn’t worth it anymore. They're backing off low-margin areas and doubling down where profits are higher and future demand is more secure.

In December, H.B. Fuller dumped its Flooring business, a unit that pulled in $160 million a year but only managed $15 million in EBITDA. The $80 million sale price won’t turn heads, but that’s not the point. The company ditched a flatlining segment, clearing out financial clutter and sparing management the headache of chasing marginal gains.

Moreover, the cash arrives just as Fuller is pulling disparate product families like Insulated Glass, Woodworking, Composites, Roofing, Building Envelope, and Infrastructure into its new Building Adhesive Solutions unit. This group produced $850 million in net revenue and $130 million in adjusted EBITDA last year, implying an operating margin north of 15% out of the gate.

These product lines used to operate in their own silos, which meant little cross-selling and a lot of redundant development. Now, with Shahbaz Malik running the show, the bet is that shared customer lists can be squeezed for more without spending more. In theory, each new sales dollar drops harder to the bottom line.

Even so, the logic is not purely internal.

Global infrastructure build-outs, tighter building-envelope standards, and the green-energy push are setting up a multi-decade demand runway. Fuller wants its glue on every pane of insulated glass, every composite bridge deck, and every rooftop solar array.

The solar segment is where the strategy gets surgical. With labor making up more than half the cost of installation, contractors obsess over anything that saves time. Fuller’s pitch, products like EternaBond DoubleStick and Adcobond D18-221, combines sealing and flashing into one step. That means less time on ladders, fewer mistakes, and faster jobs. These may be just adhesives, but the impact isn’t small. The non-curing butyl formula stays tacky and watertight for decades, and by some measures beats standard MS-polymer products. Metal Construction Association data says it lasts at least 35 years, maybe up to 60.

A lot of the post-COVID hygiene habits haven’t gone anywhere either, so demand for top-shelf tissue and towel products is still strong. Fuller’s Swift tak line, less water, more solids, is built to help converters cut emissions, trim waste, and, with the help of auto-dilution, crank out up to 20% more product. The point is that these converters run on razor-thin margins, so any efficiency boost tends to stick. And that means that the supplier who delivers it usually does too. The division only posted 1.8% organic growth, not exactly impressive at first glance, but that was enough to lift the EBITDA margin by 300 basis points to 15.6%, a sign that price discipline and throughput benefits are flowing through.

Meanwhile, if geography is destiny, Fuller’s recent footprint expansion across India, the Middle East, and Africa underscores its intent to be present where urbanization is running hottest. The new factory in Cairo, built to LEED specs and certified under FSSC 22000 and SEDEX, gives Fuller a way to move products into Europe, North America, and Africa without waiting around. While that was going on, the expanded site in Ras Al-Khaimah, beefed up by the XCHEM deal, now supplies waterproofing, HVAC, and woodworking customers up and down the Gulf. Add it up, and Fuller’s IMEA footprint includes 5 plants, 450-plus employees, and exports to more than 100 countries. This gives Fuller local cost bases but global reach. Because many governments in the region are channeling petrodollar surpluses into infrastructure and housing, the addressable market is expanding even if global GDP cools elsewhere.

All the strategy talk doesn’t mean much without numbers to back it up. The latest quarter delivered some. Adjusted earnings per share reached $1.18, ten cents above expectations and up 5% from last year. Adjusted EBITDA rose to $166 million, also a 5% gain, with margins expanding 130 basis points to 18.4%. Gross margin bumped up to 32.2%, helped by higher-value products holding their price.

Segment performance mirrors the portfolio thesis. Engineering Adhesives posted a 24% EBITDA jump and a margin of 22.9%, even though revenue slipped 0.4%, with solar-related softness masking growth in automotive, aerospace, and defense.

The freshly minted BAS unit eked out a 5% EBITDA gain on a 0.9% sales decline, suggesting early cost synergy is offsetting near-term top-line noise. Hygiene, Health and Consumable, for its part, converted modest revenue expansion into solid profit momentum.

In the meantime, cash discipline has not been sacrificed to growth, as we see that operating cash flow jumped 36% from last year to $111 million, giving management enough room to buy back 300,000 shares in the quarter and 1 million so far this year. Net-debt-to-EBITDA edged down from 3.5x to 3.4x. It’s not a low number, but it’s moving in the right direction. With a free cash flow target of $300 to $325 million for 2025, there’s room to pay off debt or pick up smaller deals.

What’s more, Fuller just raised full-year EBITDA guidance to a range of $615 to $630 million, up 4 to 6%, while forecasting adjusted EPS between $4.10 and $4.30, a 7 to 12% lift. Even the third-quarter midpoint, $170 million of EBITDA, would keep margins humming if volume headwinds persist.

Headwinds, Outlook, and Valuation for H.B. Fuller

Next, let’s look at some bearish signals in Fuller’s second-quarter report, starting with revenue, which dropped to $898.1 million, $1.9 million short of Wall Street’s target and down 2.07% from last year. Strip out the Flooring unit they dumped late last year, and revenue technically rises 2.8%, but that’s less a rebound and more a company trying to stay upright while the floor moves under it. Organic growth, which filters out deals and currency volatility, came in at just 0.4%, and volumes slipped 0.3%.

Because the weakness didn't confine itself to one trouble spot, the segment breakdown reads like a map of overlapping stress points. Engineering Adhesives, which is usually a bright spot, absorbed another 80-basis-point margin hit from the chronically choppy solar market, and even though that drag is lighter than the 120-basis-point gouge recorded a year earlier, it still hurts.

Building Adhesive Solutions fared no better since soft residential construction dulled the appetite for glass and wood glues and managed to drown out sturdier roofing demand. Meanwhile, the electronics-focused business in China paused exports for what executives think will be 1 or 2 more months, pending the translation of recent design wins into tangible orders.

Across the EIMEA region, organic revenue slipped 2% on continued construction malaise, and even in Asia–Pacific, where transportation adhesives are doing their part, the gains were wiped out by solar and electronics shortfalls, leaving the region flat year-on-year. Put differently, about half of Fuller’s end markets decelerated during the quarter, which explains why volumes went nowhere after rising 2% in the first 3 months of the fiscal year.

At the same time, external factors aren’t doing the company any favors. Management says foreign-exchange headwinds alone could shave 1 to 1.5% off full-year revenue. And while 99% of what it sells in the U.S. is made in the U.S., and 96% of what it sells in China is, well, made in China, that doesn’t shield it from the fallout of tariffs or rerouted supply chains. Such indirect shocks may take quarters to reveal themselves, which is why executives are guiding to muted volume for the rest of calendar 2025 and sprinkling their commentary with caution about geopolitical flashpoints and erratic construction demand.

Even so, the firm is not exactly hunkering down; it is just pacing its spend.

Capital spending in the first half came in at $65 million, down from $90 million a year ago, a sharp drop that points more to delayed projects than to scrapped ones. To hit the reiterated $150 million cap-ex target for the full year, Fuller has to push several hefty projects through the pipe in the back half, including further stages of its enterprise-wide SAP rollout and ongoing plant consolidation work. Management admits those two programs alone will keep capital intensity hovering around $40 million a year through at least 2026, underscoring that the transformation, while strategic, is also expensive. Layer on $3.6 million of restructuring and deal-related charges in the latest quarter, mostly clean-up costs tied to exiting Flooring, and it becomes clear that the income statement is still shouldering the friction that follows any big portfolio overhaul.

However, valuation doesn’t always care about current bruises, it leans on what’s next. On that front, things look a lot better. The stock trades at about 15.5x blended earnings, right in line with a fair-value mark of 15x, which, I admit, reads as unremarkable on its own.

But zoom out: Fuller’s five-year average is 18.7x, and the specialty-chemicals peer group hovers around 16x, and right now, the trailing multiple is 14.8x, and forward is 13.7x, both sitting below the norm.

The enterprise numbers echo the same story with forward EV-to-EBITDA is 8.3x and EV-to-EBIT is 11.8x, both below industry averages. Looking at the forward PEG ratio of 0.87x versus the sector median of 1.42x means that investors are paying less than a dollar for every dollar of expected earnings growth, even though the consensus is forecasting EPS gains of 7.1% in 2025, 13.7% in 2026, and 19.5% by 2027.

Further, because the growth story here isn’t about cutting corners, but about moving pieces around, it’s pushing money into faster-growing, higher-margin adhesive lines, rolling out SAP across the business, and cutting loose weaker assets. Again, the goal is fatter margins and a stronger bottom line once volume comes back.

The numbers seem to back it up with EPS projected to grow at a 15.85% clip, ahead of the company’s own history and most of its peers.

Dividends are moving, too. Management is targeting 5+% growth on top of the 8.4% already logged. They’re counting on cash flow holding steady, even with capex still running high.

Granted, none of those forecasts will matter if construction stays in the doldrums, if FX swings grow wilder, or if solar demand fails to rebound. Yet, the 80-basis-point solar drag has already moderated versus last year, suggesting the worst of the oversupply hangover may be easing. Likewise, the electronics slowdown in China could turn around once new designs start shipping, and roofing demand inside the Building Adhesive unit is still holding up, even as housing numbers stay weak, but between that, a focus on higher-margin products, and a self-funded investment plan, Fuller looks positioned to get through the slump without wrecking its bottom line.

Final Verdict

I rate H.B. Fuller stock a buy. With low-margin businesses gone, margins and cash flow rising, guidance moving up and the stock at roughly 14× forward earnings and a sub-1 PEG, I think the prospect of mid-teens EPS growth and dividend hikes outweighs the near-term volume, FX, and construction headwinds.

data.title: PLDT Continues To Miss The Market
data.description:
I am updating my previous analysis on PLDT Inc. (NYSE:PHI). I previously rated PLDT a sell for the following reasons:

  • Volumes were continuing to struggle due to the Philippines SIM Card registration requirement
  • Inflation was improving but ARPU wasn't growing
  • Management guidance suggested underperformance in new businesses and made the dividend risky.

Since then, PLDT has fallen 9% while the S&P 500 (SP500) has returned 21%.

With continued positive news out of Maya bank and a full-year wraparound on the SIM Card requirement, I was hopeful as I came back to look at PLDT. Unfortunately while a lot has changed, not much has changed with the core business. Most critically, the growth rate continues to miss the market growth rate significantly, showing something isn't working in the core business. Operating expense and capital expense reductions have been carrying cash flow so far, but that only lasts so long.

At a DCF generated price target of $17.00, 20% downside from today's pricing, I maintain my Sell rating on PLDT Inc.

Valuation

To generate a price target, I updated my ongoing DCF analysis using the following overarching assumptions:

  • 4% short-term revenue growth with weakness in core business slightly offset by growth in new business
  • 2% cost growth to account for carryforward of G&A efficiencies
  • 2% long-run growth rate in line with Philippine market and adjust down for cost base
  • 10% discount rate based on estimated WACC of 9.3%.

This DCF generated a price target of $17, 20% downside from today's pricing, and no change from my prior analysis.

Wall Street is much more optimistic at a price target of $32, although the stock has limited analyst coverage.

The quant rating is at a hold with weak growth and profitability offset by favorable valuation multiples. Given the sector doesn't have the same geographic exposure, valuation multiples are challenging to compare. This gives more credence to growth and profitability, which largely align with my analysis.

Growth Rate Shows Something Isn't Working

With legacy services nearing the bottom (11% of revenue per the chart below), I was expecting stronger revenue performance than a 1% growth. However, revenue excluding legacy was only up 2% with declines in mobile offset by growth in home.

The overall market is expected to grow at 4.9% through 2034. Not a single business in PLDT hit the market growth rate. This indicates to me that something serious is not working in the core business as the company loses market share across the portfolio.

Throughout the earnings call, management continued to laud 5G adoption. This makes sense, as the Philippines still has exceptionally low 5G adoption relative to other markets.

Certainly 5G adoption is ramping up rapidly (81% year-on-year), and total data traffic increased 6%. But this ramp-up has been going on for two years now and there has been no corresponding increase in ARPU or overall mobile revenue to suggest the company has a plan to monetize 5G.

Cost Base Can't Sustain Growth Forever

With revenue stagnating, cost efficiencies on both the operating expense and capital expense side have been sustaining free cash flow and the dividend to this point. S,G,&A costs were down ~10% year-over-year despite higher than normal inflation, but you can only cut so far in a telecom business with its high need for sales and infrastructure support.

The business has also brought capital intensity down from 40%+ in 2023 to 20% in the latest quarter.

20% would be theoretically sustainable based on comparable companies like Telkom Indonesia. Notice, though, that PLDT has historically been a market leader, in a bad way, in capital intensity. This tells me two things. First, they are not getting any kind of a return on investment. And two, if the strategy continues to require 5G and fiber adoption, how does cutting capital in half sustain that strategy?

Upside Potential

With legacy services largely at the bottom, the biggest upside potential comes from rightsizing growth in the core business. As discussed above, the core business excluding legacy is still not growing at the market rate. But if management strategies take hold and the business is able to meet or even exceed the market it would significantly change the valuation. Given that the current strategy has been in place for more than a year with no significant change, I do not give this a high probability.

The second, and more likely upside, is that Maya bank continues to succeed and generates outsize growth becoming a larger share of revenue and operating income. This shifts the mix away from telecom and the related high investment which could restructure the income statement and the balance sheet. Currently the business is not seeing this level of traction to significantly overtake the telecom business but it is something to keep an eye on.

Verdict

Inflation is down, SIM Card registration laws have had a full-year wraparound, market growth is forecast at close to 5%, and 5G adoption is on the upswing. With all of this, you would easily expect PLDT to be growing, but the business continues to stagnate. Even with legacy services down to 11% of total revenue, topline growth fell nearly 4 points short of the overall market.

Cost efficiencies on both operating expense and capital expense have sustained the business so far, but that has limits. While there is upside potential if management strategies to grow mobile take hold, or if Maya bank supercharges growth and restructures the company, these same strategies have been in place for over a year with limited success.

At a DCF generated price target of $17.00, 20% downside from today's pricing, I maintain my Sell rating on PLDT Inc.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
